questions.question: What is a trunk link?
options: A physical link carrying traffic from multiple VLANs between switches
A single-VLAN link to an end host
A Layer 3 routed link between two routers
A management-only link for out-of-band access
questions.explanation: A trunk link is a physical link carrying traffic from multiple VLANs between switches (or switch-to-router).

questions.question: What does 802.1Q do?
options: Inserts a 4-byte tag into the Ethernet frame between source MAC and type/length fields
Encrypts VLAN traffic on the trunk
questions.explanation: IEEE 802.1Q is the industry-standard VLAN tagging protocol; it inserts a 4-byte tag into the Ethernet frame between source MAC and type/length fields.

questions.question: What is the native VLAN on a trunk?
options: The VLAN whose frames are sent untagged; VLAN 1 by default
The VLAN used for management traffic only
The highest-priority VLAN on the trunk
The VLAN with the most hosts
questions.explanation: The native VLAN is the VLAN on a trunk whose frames are sent untagged; VLAN 1 by default; must match on both sides.

questions.question: What does DTP stand for?
questions.explanation: DTP is the Dynamic Trunking Protocol, a Cisco-proprietary protocol that negotiates trunk/access status between connected switchports.

questions.question: Which DTP mode actively tries to form a trunk?
questions.explanation: `switchport mode dynamic desirable` is the DTP mode that actively tries to form a trunk. Dynamic auto is passive.

questions.question: What is Router-on-a-Stick (ROAS)?
options: Inter-VLAN routing using a single trunk link between a switch and router, with subinterfaces for each VLAN
A dedicated router with one routed port per VLAN
A routing protocol for small sites
questions.explanation: ROAS is inter-VLAN routing using a single trunk link between a switch and router, with subinterfaces for each VLAN.

questions.question: What is an SVI?
options: A virtual Layer 3 interface on a multilayer switch representing a VLAN
A switchport assigned to a single VLAN
questions.explanation: SVI (Switch Virtual Interface) is a virtual Layer 3 interface on a multilayer switch representing a VLAN; used as gateway for inter-VLAN routing on the switch.

questions.question: What does the command `no switchport` do on a multilayer switch?
options: Converts a switchport into a Layer 3 routed port
questions.explanation: `no switchport` converts a switchport into a Layer 3 routed port on a multilayer switch.

questions.question: What is the role of a VTP server?
options: Switch that can add/modify/delete VLANs and advertises the database; stores VLANs in NVRAM
Switch that cannot modify VLANs and syncs from server
Switch that forwards VTP advertisements but doesn't sync
Switch running an encrypted VLAN database
questions.explanation: A VTP server can add/modify/delete VLANs and advertises the database; stores VLANs in NVRAM; default Cisco mode.

questions.question: What field in the 802.1Q tag is always 0x8100?
questions.explanation: TPID (Tag Protocol Identifier) is always 0x8100 in an 802.1Q tag, marking the frame as dot1q-tagged.

questions.question: Which command restricts a trunk to only carry VLANs 10 and 30?
questions.explanation: `switchport trunk allowed vlan 10,30` restricts the trunk to only these VLANs.

questions.question: What causes a native VLAN mismatch problem?
options: Two trunk endpoints disagreeing on the native VLAN; causes traffic to land in the wrong VLAN
Using different trunk encapsulations on either end
Running different IOS versions on the switches
Forgetting to assign an IP address to the SVI
questions.explanation: A native VLAN mismatch is a misconfiguration where two trunk endpoints disagree on the native VLAN; causes traffic to land in the wrong VLAN.

questions.question: Which legacy Cisco-proprietary trunk encapsulation is largely replaced by 802.1Q?
questions.explanation: ISL (Inter-Switch Link) is the legacy Cisco-proprietary trunk encapsulation, largely replaced by 802.1Q.

questions.question: Which command disables DTP frames on an interface?
questions.explanation: `switchport nonegotiate` disables DTP frames on the interface.

questions.question: What is the danger of VTP revision number behavior?
options: An old switch with a higher revision number can wipe out VLANs in the domain
Revision numbers are encrypted and can't be audited
Revision numbers don't sync between server and client
questions.explanation: VTP revision number increments on every VLAN change; switches sync to the highest revision in the domain — a danger is an old switch with higher revision can wipe out VLANs.
